images.caption: Figure 1:Reversing the teacher signal turns self-distillation into valuable exploration. (a) Training reward on Qwen3-4B-Base underGRPO, upweightingteacher-predictedtokens on correct rollouts, and upweightingself-driventokens (RLRT). (b) Mean avg@16 score over six math benchmarks (AIME24/25/26, HMMT26, AMC23, MATH500) across four Qwen3 backbones. RLRT consistently outperforms baselines significantly. Full results are in Tables1and3. We skipped detailed comparisons with SDPO/SRPO on the base models because they collapsed early during training (AppendixF.2).

Figure 2:Critical positions and explore/exploit directions.Token shading shows the position-level asymmetryD¯t=KL​(PSt∥PTt)\bar{D}_{t}=\mathrm{KL}(P_{S}^{t}\,\|\,P_{T}^{t}). At each critical position (right panels), candidate tokens are taken as the union of the teacher’s and student’s top-100 tokens; we display the top four with the largestPSt−PTtP_{S}^{t}-P_{T}^{t}(green,D^t>0\hat{D}_{t}>0) and the top four with the largestPTt−PStP_{T}^{t}-P_{S}^{t}(pink,D^t<0\hat{D}_{t}<0).

Figure 3:Reasoning markers in the explore/exploit population.(a) Volcano scatter of linguistic tokens:xx-axis is the polarizationlog2⁡(nexplore/nexploit)\log_{2}(n_{\text{explore}}/n_{\text{exploit}}),yy-axis is the total countlog10⁡(nexplore+nexploit)\log_{10}(n_{\text{explore}}+n_{\text{exploit}}). Highlighted points (green==explore,pink==exploit) are categorized discourse markers; grey points are uncategorized tokens. (b) Per-marker polarization for these markers, sorted from most exploit-leaning (left) to most explore-leaning (right).

Figure 5:Training score curves across four backbones (Qwen3-4B-Base, Qwen3-8B-Base,
Qwen3-4B-Instruct, Qwen3-8B). RLRT achieves faster exploration and
higher training scores in all settings.
Figure 6:Reflection injected atmax_kl(■\blacksquare),random(∙\bullet), ormin_kl(▲\blacktriangle).(a)flip→\toRon hard subset;(b)flip→\toWon easy subset.

images.caption: Figure 10:Additional example of critical positions and explore/exploit directions, complementing Figure2. Token shading shows the position-level asymmetryD¯t=KL​(PSt∥PTt)\bar{D}_{t}=\mathrm{KL}(P_{S}^{t}\,\|\,P_{T}^{t}). At the highlighted critical position, candidates are taken as the union of the teacher’s and student’s top-100 tokens; we display the top four with the largestPSt−PTtP_{S}^{t}-P_{T}^{t}(green,D^t>0\hat{D}_{t}>0,explore) and the top four with the largestPTt−PStP_{T}^{t}-P_{S}^{t}(pink,D^t<0\hat{D}_{t}<0,exploit).
Figure 11:Training reward (left) and response length (right) on Qwen3-8B-Base.SDPOcollapses quickly: its reward drops while response length blows up.

Figure 12:Full-trajectory heatmap ofD¯t\bar{D}_{t}on the first example rollout. Critical positions (green) are sparse and concentrate at decision points, while long routine stretches (pink) carry little signal.
